name: Waymo
short_description: A fully autonomous driving system that operates a robotaxi fleet without a human driver.
long_description: The Waymo Driver is the autonomous driving system behind Waymo One, a commercial robotaxi service that carries paying passengers with no human behind the wheel. It fuses lidar, radar, and a 29-camera suite with onboard compute and HD maps to perceive, predict, and plan in real city traffic, drawing on hundreds of millions of real-world and billions of simulated miles. Spun out of Google's self-driving car project (begun 2009) as an Alphabet company in 2016, it is one of the first physical AI systems to move ordinary people through everyday urban life at scale, making autonomy a routine part of daily transportation.

images.caption: A Waymo-operated Jaguar I-Pace autonomous vehicle crossing the 4th Street Bridge in San Francisco, May 2025.
images.alt_text: A white Jaguar I-Pace electric SUV fitted with roof-mounted and bumper sensor pods for autonomous driving, seen from the front three-quarter angle on a city street.

sources.title: Self-Driving Car Technology for a Reliable Ride - Waymo Driver
sources.access_notes: Official page describing the Waymo Driver sensor suite (lidar, radar, 29 cameras, compute) and how it perceives, predicts, and plans.

sources.title: Waymo
sources.access_notes: Background on Waymo's history: Google self-driving project (2009), spun out as Waymo under Alphabet in 2016, Waymo One commercial launch in Phoenix in 2018.

interactions.title: A judgement-free space at 20 mph
interactions.description: Riders take phone calls, cry, rehearse, or sit in silence with their own playlist on — no driver to perform for. Waymo says people logged over 3.8 million hours in 2025 of this 'trusted space' to catch up on work or relax.

interactions.title: Honk the car to find it
interactions.description: A blind or low-vision rider taps 'Honk the horn' or 'Play melody' in the app and walks toward the sound, locating the right car at a busy pickup without seeing it.

interactions.title: Set the cabin before it arrives
interactions.description: You save temperature, legroom, and audio preferences and stream your own Spotify or YouTube Music in the car — but try any other control and the screen reminds you it's off-limits.

interactions.title: The 4 a.m. honking chorus
interactions.description: Driverless cars parked overnight in a SoMa lot kept nosing close to one another and triggering courtesy honks, beeping at each other for weeks until neighbors started filming and complaining to the city.

interactions.title: Disabled with a single traffic cone
interactions.description: Activists from Safe Street Rebel discovered that setting an orange cone on the hood spooks the car's safety system into freezing in place, and turned it into a protest ritual against robotaxis on city streets.

interactions.title: The 427-rides-a-year power rider
interactions.description: One anonymous San Franciscan took 427 Waymo trips in 2025 — about 2,700 miles, roughly eight full days in the back seat — turning the robotaxi into a daily personal chauffeur.

interactions.title: When the construction zone breaks it
interactions.description: A rider filmed their Waymo accelerating through a freeway work zone and being trailed by police; the viral clip pushed Waymo to pull all freeway service in four metros while it retrained for construction.

interactions.title: Narrate the streets for a rider who can't see them
interactions.description: Building on the car's existing 'descriptive' in-car audio mode and its lidar-and-camera scene understanding, the cabin could narrate passing landmarks and turns aloud — a richer travelogue for blind riders than the current minimal trip cues.
